Summary: Sox9 is an astrocyte specifier that is specifically expressed in naïve astrocytes and upregulated in reactive astrocytes after brain or spinal cord injury. In naïve animals Sox9 maintains basal Hk1 levels. Nerve injury triggers Sox9 phosphorylation that enhances Sox9 nuclear translocation and transcriptional activity. Upstream kinases implicated include cAMP–PKA, MAPK, and PI3K/Akt. Phosphorylated Sox9 causes pathological Hk1 upregulation and aberrant glycolytic flux in astrocytes. Phosphorylated Sox9 contributes to the emergence of the Hk1-mediated neuroinflammatory astrocyte cluster 1. Phosphorylated Sox9 induces Astro 2, which represents a property with neurodegenerative and demyelinating nature. Aberrant Hk1 activation drives high-rate astrocytic glycolysis and leads to excessive lactate production from heightened astrocytic glycolysis. Excess lactate remodels histones via lactylation (H3K9la) and promotes pro-inflammatory and neurotoxic transcriptional modules that reduce beneficial astrocyte populations and induce pathogenic astrocyte properties. The pathogenic astrocyte changes contribute to neuropathic pain. Targeted modulation of the Sox9–Hk1–H3K9la axis specifically dampens neuroinflammatory astrocyte subsets and promotes long-lasting pain relief.
Reference: Chen, Y., Liao, Y., Zhu, H., Wu, Z., Wan, Y., Feng, X., Fan, C., Zhan, D., Cai, H., Cheung, M., Cheung, C. W., & Liu, J. A. (2025). Sox9 regulation of hexokinase 1 controls neuroinflammatory astrocyte subtypes in a rat model of neuropathic pain. Nat Commun, 16(1), 10249. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-025-65092-5

Summary: SOX9 labels astrocyte-like cells in diffuse grade II IDH1‑mutant oligodendrogliomas and astrocytomas and serves as a marker of astrocytic-lineage cells. In six examined IDH‑mutant diffuse grade II tumors OLIG1+ and SOX9+ cells formed two largely nonoverlapping populations. In one astrocytoma the majority of SOX9+ cells lacked nuclear ATRX staining indicating they were tumoral. In one oligodendroglioma about 15% of SOX9+ cells showed faint IDH1 R132H staining possibly reflecting lower IDH1 expression. SOX9+ cells coexpressed APOE, CRYAB, ID4, and KCNN3 and exhibited higher BMP, NOTCH1, and MAPK pathway readouts including BMP4, HEY1/2, p‑SMAD1/5, and p‑ERK. In primary cultures and a new cell line NOTCH1 activation reduced oligodendrocytic markers and IDH1 while upregulating APOE, CRYAB, HEY1/2, and KCNN3 and decreasing proliferation. NOTCH1 activation induced strong SOX9 in non‑tumoral oligodendrocytic cells. BMP treatment reduced OLIG1/2 and increased CRYAB and NOGGIN. The SOX9+ astrocytic subpopulation is distinct from OLIG1+ cells and its role in pathology remains open.
Reference: Augustus, M., Pineau, D., Aimond, F., Azar, S., Lecca, D., Scamps, F., Muxel, S., Darlix, A., Ritchie, W., Gozé, C., Rigau, V., Duffau, H., & Hugnot, J. P. (2021). Identification of CRYAB+ KCNN3+ SOX9+ Astrocyte-Like and EGFR+ PDGFRA+ OLIG1+ Oligodendrocyte-Like Tumoral Cells in Diffuse IDH1-Mutant Gliomas and Implication of NOTCH1 Signalling in Their Genesis. Cancers (Basel), 13(9), 2107. https://doi.org/10.3390/cancers13092107

Summary: In the regions studied, cells labeled with the pan-astrocytic marker SOX9 were 100% positive for Rab6A. The passage states that Rab6A/SOX9 double staining would further confirm the recent introduction of SOX9 as a specific pan-astrocytic marker and would in parallel support the conclusion that Rab6A is also a pan-astrocytic marker.
Reference: Melzer, L., Freiman, T. M., & Derouiche, A. (2021). Rab6A as a Pan-Astrocytic Marker in Mouse and Human Brain, and Comparison with Other Glial Markers (GFAP, GS, Aldh1L1, SOX9). Cells, 10(1), E72. https://doi.org/10.3390/cells10010072

Summary: In NPC cultures, Sox9 promotes astrocyte and oligodendrocyte differentiation and suppresses neuronal differentiation, and Sox9 knockdown produces the opposite effects. In NPCs, knockdown of Sox9 markedly attenuates Zbtb20-driven astrocyte differentiation and reverses Zbtb20's inhibition of neuronal differentiation, indicating Sox9 is required for Zbtb20's promotion of astrocytogenesis. Conversely, Zbtb20 knockdown suppresses Sox9-driven astrocyte induction. Overexpression of Zbtb20 does not raise Sox9 mRNA and Sox9 overexpression does not increase Zbtb20 mRNA. Sox9 slightly increases the fraction of Zbtb20-expressing cells and Zbtb20 and Sox9 may act together or in parallel. In the embryonic spinal cord, Sox9 together with NFIA is implicated in astrocyte specification. Conditional knockout of Sox9 in the developing spinal cord produces defects in specification of both astrocytes and oligodendrocytes and indicates that Sox9 and NFIA do not exclusively mark precursors committed to the astrocytic fate. Zbtb20 directly represses Brn2 expression in the neocortex; Zbtb20, together with Sox9 and NFIA, promotes astrocytogenesis in part through repression of Brn2.
Reference: Nagao, M., Ogata, T., Sawada, Y., & Gotoh, Y. (2016). Zbtb20 promotes astrocytogenesis during neocortical development. Nat Commun, 7, 11102. https://doi.org/10.1038/ncomms11102

Summary: Astrocytes differentiate via nuclear factor I-A, Sox9, and Notch pathways.
Reference: Vellucci, L., Mazza, B., Barone, A., Nasti, A., De Simone, G., Iasevoli, F., & de Bartolomeis, A. (2025). The Role of Astrocytes in the Molecular Pathophysiology of Schizophrenia: Between Neurodevelopment and Neurodegeneration. Biomolecules, 15(5), 615. https://doi.org/10.3390/biom15050615
